rule_name: Agency Sunset Rule Mandate (Executive Order 14270, "Zero-Based Regulatory Budgeting to Unleash American Energy")
agency: White House / OMB (directs EPA, DOE, DOI, FERC, NRC, Army Corps of Engineers, BLM, BOEM)
status: Fully Enacted (in effect; individual agency sunset rules being implemented through 2026)
original_rule_summary: No prior rule -- this is a new process mandate targeting energy-related regulations under statutes including the Clean Air Act, Federal Power Act, Natural Gas Act, Atomic Energy Act, Endangered Species Act, and appliance efficiency standards.
what_changed: Directs covered agencies (EPA, DOE, FERC, NRC, DOI bureaus, Army Corps, BLM, BOEM) to issue a 'sunset rule' by September 30, 2025 (later extended to September 30, 2026 for some agencies) inserting a 'Conditional Sunset Date' into covered existing regulations -- one year after the sunset rule's effective date, unless the agency affirmatively extends it (up to 5 more years at a time, following a public comment opportunity). All new energy-related regulations must include a sunset date no more than 5 years out. If an agency does not act to extend a regulation before its sunset date, the regulation automatically expires and is removed from the Code of Federal Regulations -- no additional agency action or public process is required for the expiration itself.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Signed EO 14270 personally on April 9, 2025, as one of three deregulatory orders issued that day (alongside the 'Directing the Repeal of Unlawful Regulations' memorandum, tracked separately in this database).

corporate_beneficiaries: Energy producers and regulated industries broadly across the covered statutes -- a structural mandate rather than a single-sector rollback

estimated_health_impact.summary: Severe systemic/structural risk: creates 'rolling regulatory vacuums' where protections automatically expire by default unless an agency proactively acts to renew them within the window -- inverting the normal presumption that a regulation stays in force until affirmatively repealed. Affected regulations may include power plant emissions standards, appliance efficiency standards, Endangered Species Act provisions, and oil/gas leasing rules.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: No quantified health impact figure exists for the EO itself, since it is a process mechanism rather than a rule targeting a specific pollutant or practice -- and as of mid-2026, agencies are still in the process of identifying which specific regulations are 'covered' and issuing their individual sunset rules (e.g. NRC's Dec. 2025 proposal found most of its regulations could NOT be sunset because they're statutorily required for safety, and only sunset a small number of unused/obsolete rules). The real-world impact will depend heavily on which agencies let which specific regulations lapse -- track individual agency sunset-rule outcomes as they finalize.

confidence_note: Strong 'process is the harm' entry, similar to EO 14192 but energy-specific and more severe in mechanism -- automatic expiration by default, rather than requiring agencies to affirmatively act to repeal. Worth noting the NRC's early implementation actually sunset very few regulations (found most were statutorily protected), which is a useful nuance: the mechanism is structurally risky, but agency-by-agency outcomes so far are mixed, not uniformly severe.

sources.name: Beveridge & Diamond — Tracking Update: Trump Administration's Latest Deregulatory Actions
American Public Power Association — Trump Issues Executive Orders Tied to Energy, Environmental Regulations
American Nuclear Society — NRC proposes rule changes in response to EO

short_summary: Energy/environmental rules now auto-expire after 1 year unless agencies act to renew them.

rule_name: CFPB Payday Lending Underwriting Rollback
original_rule_summary: 2017 CFPB rule (under Director Richard Cordray) requiring payday and short-term title lenders to verify a borrower's actual ability to repay a loan before issuing it -- an underwriting requirement developed over 5 years of CFPB research, aimed at preventing debt-trap cycles from loans with APRs often exceeding 390%.
what_changed: The Trump-era CFPB proposed and later finalized removal of the ability-to-repay ('underwriting') provision, while leaving in place separate provisions restricting how lenders can debit a borrower's bank account. CFPB's own senior officials stated the rescission stemmed from a view that there wasn't enough evidence the practice was 'unfair and abusive' -- despite the agency's own prior 5-year research record concluding the opposite.

primary_proponent.name: Mick Mulvaney
primary_proponent.note: Initiated the delay and rollback process starting in 2018; publicly called the CFPB a 'sick, sad joke' and had proposed abolishing it as a congressman. Documented as having accepted campaign donations from payday lenders and dropping CFPB enforcement actions against installment lenders during his tenure. The rule was finalized under his successor, Kathy Kraninger, in 2020.

corporate_beneficiaries: Payday and short-term title lenders, including Select Management Resources, Advance America, and Check Into Cash, several of which donated to Trump's inaugural committee

estimated_health_impact.summary: Indirect: debt-trap cycles correlate with acute psychological anxiety, depression, and stress-related physical ailments. CFPB's own estimate: the rescinded ability-to-repay provisions were projected to save consumers (and cost lenders) $7 billion per year -- a strong, agency-originated figure for debate use.

figures.metric: CFPB's own estimate of annual consumer savings from the rescinded provisions
figures.source: CFPB, cited by Banking Dive

estimated_health_impact.caveat: The $7B figure is a financial-savings estimate, not a direct health/mortality figure -- the health linkage (debt-trap stress correlating with mental and physical health harm) is drawn from general research on financial stress, not a rule-specific health study.

confidence_note: Strong sourcing on the regulatory action, the proponent (Mulvaney, with documented conflicts of interest), and a real agency-originated dollar figure. The mental/physical health linkage should be framed as 'debt-trap stress is well-documented to correlate with anxiety and physical health harm' rather than implying a rule-specific quantified health study exists.

sources.name: NPR — Consumer Protection Bureau Aims To Roll Back Rule For Payday Lending
Banking Dive — CFPB rolls back 'ability-to-repay' portion of payday lending rule
Consumer Reports Advocacy — Actions Taken By Acting CFPB Director Mick Mulvaney

short_summary: Payday lenders no longer required to verify borrowers can actually repay loans.

rule_name: Clean Power Plan repeal and replacement with the Affordable Clean Energy (ACE) Rule
original_rule_summary: 2015 Obama-era EPA rule setting the first-ever national carbon dioxide emission limits for existing power plants, aimed at cutting power-sector greenhouse gas emissions roughly 32% below 2005 levels by 2030.
what_changed: EPA repealed the Clean Power Plan outright and replaced it with the much weaker Affordable Clean Energy Rule, which set no binding numeric emissions targets and instead directed states to require only modest efficiency upgrades ('heat rate improvements') at individual coal plants, explicitly rejecting the prior rule's system-wide approach of shifting generation toward cleaner sources.

primary_proponent.name: Andrew Wheeler
primary_proponent.role: EPA Administrator (finalized the rule; the repeal process began under predecessor Scott Pruitt)
primary_proponent.note: Wheeler, a former coal lobbyist, signed the final ACE Rule; Pruitt had initiated the Clean Power Plan repeal process in 2017.

corporate_beneficiaries: Coal-fired power plant operators
Coal mining companies

estimated_health_impact.summary: EPA's own impact analysis found the ACE Rule replacement would result in more air pollution and premature deaths than either the Clean Power Plan or a full do-nothing baseline in some scenarios, by allowing individual coal plants to run longer and more often even after efficiency upgrades ('the rebound effect').

figures.metric: EPA's own estimate of additional premature deaths per year by 2030 under ACE vs. stronger alternatives
figures.source: EPA Regulatory Impact Analysis for the ACE Rule (2019)

estimated_health_impact.caveat: This is EPA's own regulatory impact analysis comparing ACE to alternative approaches, not an outside advocacy estimate -- a notably strong data point since the agency implementing the rollback produced the mortality estimate itself.

confidence_note: Very strong for debate: the mortality estimate came from EPA's own regulatory impact analysis, not an opposing advocacy group. This is close to an admission-against-interest and should be framed that way.

sources.name: Brookings — The Trump administration's major environmental deregulations
Wikipedia — Environmental policy of the first Donald Trump administration

short_summary: Replaced binding power-plant carbon caps with a weaker efficiency-only standard.

rule_name: Coal Combustion Residuals (Coal Ash) Disposal Rule Rollback
status: Delayed/Paused (deadline extension finalized 2026-02-10) + Proposed Repeal (broader weakening proposed 2026-04-09, not yet finalized)
original_rule_summary: EPA's 2024 'Legacy CCR Rule' extended federal coal ash monitoring, cleanup, and closure requirements to hundreds of older, previously-unregulated coal ash disposal sites -- 'legacy' impoundments and 'CCR Management Units' (CCRMUs) -- covering roughly half of all coal ash waste ever generated in the US, following a 2015 rule that only covered active sites. EPA identified over 1,000 dumpsites across 42 states requiring monitoring, closure, or remediation.
what_changed: In a Feb. 10, 2026 final rule, EPA extended deadlines for ~110 coal-fired generating units and 189 disposal sites: legacy-site evaluation/cleanup pushed from roughly 2029 to February 2032, and groundwater monitoring pushed from May 2028 to February 2031 -- a roughly 3-year delay. Separately, on April 9, 2026, EPA proposed a much broader rule that would exempt the majority of these dumpsites from federal oversight entirely, alter the definition of 'beneficial use,' and let permit authorities make site-specific determinations on groundwater monitoring points and cleanup levels.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: Stated the changes reflect 'EPA's commitment to restoring American energy dominance.'

corporate_beneficiaries: Owners of coal-fired power plants and disposal sites, who EPA's own final rule estimates will save $8.1-30 million per year from the deadline extension alone

estimated_health_impact.summary: Coal ash contains mercury, cadmium, chromium, and arsenic. EPA's own final rule (Feb. 2026) states explicitly that delaying the compliance deadlines 'may increase health risks for children and other populations.' EPA's own 2024 rule justification found legacy impoundments, inactive landfills, and CCRMUs 'posed significant risks to human health and the environment' requiring federal oversight -- the April 2026 proposal would exempt the majority of these same sites from that oversight.

figures.metric: Coal-fired generating units affected by the February 2026 deadline extension
figures.source: US EPA final rule, cited by Utility Dive

figures.metric: Coal ash disposal sites affected
figures.source: US EPA final rule, cited by Utility Dive

figures.metric: Annual savings to landfill owners from the deadline delay
figures.source: US EPA final rule, cited by Utility Dive

estimated_health_impact.caveat: The February 2026 deadline-extension rule is finalized; the broader April 2026 proposal to exempt most sites from oversight entirely is still proposed, not finalized (comment period extended to June 29, 2026). Frame the two as separate but related actions -- one enacted, one pending.

confidence_note: Unusually strong entry: EPA's own final rule text contains a direct admission that the delay 'may increase health risks for children,' which is about as close to an admission-against-interest as this tracker contains. Excellent for debate use precisely because it's not an outside estimate. [Rechecked 2026-07-06: no material change to the finalized Feb 10, 2026 deadline extension. The broader April 9, 2026 weakening proposal remains pending, not finalized -- its comment period has been extended/reopened at least once since (through late June 2026 per EELP tracker).]

sources.name: Utility Dive — EPA extends coal ash landfill monitoring, cleanup deadlines
Utility Dive — EPA proposes weakening power plant coal ash protections
Harvard EELP — Coal Ash Rule tracker
Sen. Dick Durbin — Senators' letter opposing the EPA coal ash rollback

short_summary: Delayed coal ash cleanup deadlines ~3 years; broader exemption for most sites still pending.

rule_name: Deregulatory Fast-Track Memo ("Directing the Repeal of Unlawful Regulations")
agency: White House / OMB (directs all executive departments and agencies)
original_rule_summary: No prior rule -- builds on Executive Order 14219 ('Ensuring Lawful Governance...'), issued Feb. 19, 2025, which had directed agencies to identify 'unlawful and potentially unlawful' regulations within a 60-day review period.
what_changed: This Presidential Memorandum instructs agencies to repeal regulations they determine are 'facially unlawful' without going through the normal notice-and-comment rulemaking process, by invoking the Administrative Procedure Act's 'good cause' exception (normally reserved for genuine emergencies). It targets regulations agencies believe conflict with 10 specific recent Supreme Court decisions, including Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (2024, overturning Chevron deference), West Virginia v. EPA (2022, establishing the 'major questions doctrine'), and Sackett v. EPA (2023, narrowing WOTUS). Within 30 days after the review period, agencies must submit a one-page summary to OMB's Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA) for each regulation identified as unlawful, then move to repeal.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Issued the memorandum personally on April 9, 2025, the same day as EO 14270 (the sunset rule mandate, tracked separately) and an EO on showerhead water pressure standards.

corporate_beneficiaries: Regulated industries broadly across all sectors where agencies determine a rule is 'unlawful' under the cited Supreme Court decisions -- a cross-cutting structural mandate rather than a sector-specific rollback

estimated_health_impact.summary: Severe systemic risk by design: bypassing notice-and-comment eliminates the primary formal mechanism by which public health advocates, toxicologists, affected communities, and other stakeholders can submit evidence or objections before a rule is repealed. This is a procedural harm distinct from any single rule's substantive content -- it changes how rules can be undone, not just which rules.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: No quantified health impact figure exists for the memo itself, since it is a process mechanism enabling repeal of other rules, not a rule targeting a specific pollutant or practice. Its harm is best argued as procedural (loss of public input) rather than via a body-count claim. Downstream rules repealed under its authority may have their own quantified figures in other entries of this tracker.

confidence_note: Strong 'process is the harm' entry, similar in category to EO 14192 and the sunset rule mandate. The specific mechanism here -- invoking APA 'good cause' to skip notice-and-comment based on an agency's own legal judgment that a rule is 'unlawful' -- is a distinct and arguably more aggressive procedural shortcut than either of the other two structural entries, since it removes public input entirely rather than just accelerating repeal volume or adding expiration dates.

sources.name: Beveridge & Diamond — Tracking Update: Trump Administration's Latest Deregulatory Actions
Latham & Watkins — Trump Administration Pursues Deregulation in a Trio of Orders
American Public Power Association — Trump Issues Executive Orders Tied to Energy, Environmental Regulations

short_summary: Lets agencies repeal rules they deem 'unlawful' by skipping public notice-and-comment.

rule_name: Dodd-Frank Asset Threshold Amendment (Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act / S.2155)
status: Fully Repealed (legislative rollback of Dodd-Frank provisions)
original_rule_summary: Post-2008 Dodd-Frank Act (2010) required banks with $50 billion or more in assets to undergo enhanced Federal Reserve oversight: company-run stress tests, enhanced liquidity requirements, resolution plans ('living wills'), and heightened supervision, on the theory that failures at this scale posed systemic risk.
what_changed: S.2155 raised the asset threshold for enhanced prudential standards from $50 billion to $250 billion, exempting all but the largest US banks. Banks with $50-100B in assets were exempted immediately; banks with $100-250B were exempted after 18 months (with Federal Reserve discretion to re-impose standards). This was the most significant legislative rollback of Dodd-Frank since its passage.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Primary Senate sponsor and drafter was Sen. Mike Crapo (R-ID), Senate Banking Committee Chair. Passed Senate 67-31 (bipartisan) and House 258-159.

corporate_beneficiaries: Regional and super-regional banks with $50-250B in assets, including Comerica, Zions, BB&T, SunTrust, Fifth Third, and Silicon Valley Bank

estimated_health_impact.summary: Indirect/systemic: increases financial system volatility risk, which correlates with mental health and economic-stability harms during downturns rather than a direct physical health pathway. This is not purely theoretical -- the Roosevelt Institute directly traces mechanisms of the 2023 Silicon Valley Bank collapse to the stress-test and liquidity exemptions this law created for banks in the $50-250B range.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: No formal quantified mortality/illness estimate exists for this rollback, as is typical for financial (rather than environmental/health) regulation -- the strength of this entry is the documented causal chain to an actual bank failure (SVB, 2023), not a modeled health figure.

confidence_note: This is legislation, not a unilateral executive rollback -- note the mechanism is different from most other entries in this tracker. Strongest framing: cite the direct causal chain to the Silicon Valley Bank collapse as a real-world materialized harm, rather than an abstract 'increases risk' claim.

sources.name: Wikipedia — Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act
Yale Journal on Regulation — Significant Rollback of Dodd-Frank Signed Into Law
Roosevelt Institute — How 2018 Regulatory Rollbacks Set the Stage for the Silicon Valley Bank Collapse

short_summary: Exempted regional banks under $250B in assets from strict post-2008 oversight.

rule_name: Overtime Salary Threshold Expansion Rescission
agency: Department of Labor (Wage and Hour Division)
original_rule_summary: In April 2024, the Biden administration's DOL finalized a rule raising the Fair Labor Standards Act white-collar overtime exemption salary threshold in two phases: to $844/week ($43,888/year) effective July 1, 2024, and to $1,128/week ($58,656/year) effective January 1, 2025, with automatic updates every three years thereafter. The highly-compensated-employee exemption threshold was set to rise to $151,164/year. The rule was projected to make roughly 4.3 million additional salaried workers newly eligible for overtime pay.
what_changed: Two federal judges in the Eastern District of Texas struck down the 2024 rule before its second scheduled increase took effect, vacating it on the grounds that DOL had over-weighted salary level relative to job duties in defining the exemption. The Trump DOL declined to continue defending the rule on appeal and withdrew the government's appeals; the Fifth Circuit dismissed the case on May 5, 2026 without ruling on the merits. On May 14, 2026, DOL published a technical amendment in the Federal Register formally rescinding the 2024 rule from the Code of Federal Regulations, effective May 15, 2026, and reinstating the salary thresholds set during Trump's first term in 2019: $684/week ($35,568/year) for the standard exemption and $107,432/year for highly compensated employees. In practice this formalized what had already been happening since the district court rulings, since DOL's Wage and Hour Division had been enforcing the 2019 thresholds since the 2024 rule was vacated.

primary_proponent.name: Andrew Rogers
primary_proponent.role: Administrator, DOL Wage and Hour Division
primary_proponent.note: Signed and publicly announced the technical amendment, framing it as ensuring the exemption's 'duties, salary basis, and salary level requirements' are 'clearly framed' -- i.e., restoring duties-based analysis over the salary-level test the 2024 rule relied on more heavily.

corporate_beneficiaries: Employers across all sectors who would otherwise have had to reclassify salaried employees between roughly $35,568 and $58,656/year as overtime-eligible, or raise their salaries above the new threshold to preserve exempt status -- disproportionately affecting labor-intensive, lower-margin industries such as retail, hospitality, and food service where many managerial-title but modestly-paid positions cluster in that salary band.

estimated_health_impact.summary: Not a health-impact rule; the relevant harm is economic -- lost overtime eligibility and pay for millions of salaried workers who would have qualified for time-and-a-half compensation under the rescinded 2024 threshold.

figures.metric: Workers projected to gain overtime eligibility under the rescinded 2024 rule
figures.source: Sen. Bernie Sanders' office fact sheet on the Restoring Overtime Pay Act of 2026, citing DOL's own 2024 rule projections

figures.metric: Current standard exemption salary threshold (restored 2019 level)
figures.source: DOL Wage and Hour Division news release, May 14, 2026

figures.metric: Threshold the 2024 rule would have set as of Jan 1, 2025
figures.source: DOL Wage and Hour Division news release, May 14, 2026; Ogletree legal alert

figures.metric: Share of full-time salaried workers currently eligible for overtime by salary alone
figures.source: Sen. Whitehouse press release, citing DOL historical data

estimated_health_impact.caveat: DOL's stated rationale, echoed by the Fifth Circuit's earlier reasoning on the 2019 rule, is that Congress intended the white-collar exemption to turn primarily on job duties rather than a compensation test, and that the 2024 rule's salary jump effectively substituted a bright-line pay threshold for that duties analysis -- a view two federal judges accepted when vacating the rule. Critics, including the bill's Senate sponsors, argue the rescission simply strips overtime protection from workers who would have newly qualified, and that the reinstated $684/week threshold has not been adjusted for inflation since 2019. Both sides agree the practical effect since late 2024 has been the same: employers have been operating under the 2019 thresholds since the district court rulings, so this technical amendment formalizes an already-realized status quo rather than changing day-to-day practice by itself.

confidence_note: High confidence throughout. The rescission, its effective date, and both salary threshold figures are drawn directly from DOL's own May 14, 2026 news release and corroborated by multiple independent legal-alert summaries (Foley Hoag, Ogletree, Stein Adler Dabah & Zelkowitz). The 4.3-million-worker figure originates from DOL's own 2024 rule impact projections, cited secondhand via a Senate office fact sheet; treated as a solid but administration-sourced estimate rather than an independently audited figure.

sources.name: U.S. Department of Labor -- Technical amendment news release, May 14, 2026
Foley Hoag LLP -- DOL Reverses Biden-Era Overtime Rule: What Employers Need to Know
Ogletree Deakins -- Trump Administration Rescinds 2024 DOL White Collar Overtime Expansion
Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse press release -- Whitehouse & Reed Seek to Reverse Trump Denial of OT Pay
Sen. Bernie Sanders' office -- Restoring Overtime Pay Act of 2026 Fact Sheet

rule_name: 2009 Endangerment Finding rescission
original_rule_summary: 2009 EPA scientific and legal finding, based on peer-reviewed climate research, that greenhouse gases endanger public health and welfare -- this finding is the legal foundation obligating EPA to regulate greenhouse gas emissions from vehicles and stationary sources under the Clean Air Act.
what_changed: EPA finalized the rescission of the 2009 Endangerment Finding on February 12, 2026 (published Feb 18, effective April 20, 2026), removing the legal foundation for EPA's authority to regulate greenhouse gas emissions from motor vehicles under CAA Section 202(a). As part of the same final action, EPA also repealed all GHG emission standards for light-, medium-, and heavy-duty vehicles and engines (model years 2012 through 2027+), eliminating future measurement, control, and reporting obligations for manufacturers -- including retroactively for vehicles already manufactured. EPA's final rule notably dropped its earlier proposed climate-science-based rationale entirely, resting the rescission purely on statutory interpretation (that CAA Section 202(a) does not authorize regulation based on global climate change concerns) and the major questions doctrine. NOTE: this action substantially overlaps with the separately-tracked 'ldmv-ghg-standards-repeal-trump2' entry in this database -- both describe the same vehicle-standards repeal, which occurred as part of this single final rule rather than as two separate actions. Recommend merging or cross-referencing these two entries.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: EPA itself described this as 'the single largest deregulatory action in U.S. history.' Trump directed EPA to evaluate the finding's legality via executive order on his first day in office (Jan 2025); Zeldin formally recommended reconsideration by March 2025.

corporate_beneficiaries: Fossil fuel producers and fossil-fuel-dependent power generators broadly, as the finding underlies multiple downstream emissions rules
Vehicle and engine manufacturers, who EPA states no longer have future GHG measurement/control/reporting obligations for any highway vehicle, including past model years

estimated_health_impact.summary: EPA's own framing states the rescission will save Americans over $1.3 trillion, though this figure is not broken down between compliance-cost savings and forgone health/climate benefits. Because the endangerment finding is the legal foundation for multiple downstream rules, its fuller health impact is best understood as unlocking rollback of everything built on top of it (vehicle GHG standards already repealed in the same action; power plant standards likely to follow under the same legal theory).

figures.metric: EPA's own claimed total savings from this action
figures.source: US EPA, Fact Sheet: EPA Delivers Single Largest Deregulatory Action in U.S. History

estimated_health_impact.caveat: The $1.3 trillion figure is EPA's own claim and is not disaggregated into cost-savings vs. forgone-benefits components in the sourcing found -- treat as an agency-asserted total, not an independently verified net-benefit calculation. Multiple health and environmental organizations dispute the underlying reasoning; see litigation status below.

confidence_note: This entry now describes a FINALIZED action, not a proposal -- a major upgrade in verifiability from the prior 'Proposed Repeal' status. Litigation is active and significant: 17 health/environmental organizations and a separate youth-plaintiff group (18 individuals) filed petitions for review at the D.C. Circuit on Feb 18, 2026; a California-led multistate AG coalition challenge was also expected. A 25-state coalition led by West Virginia and Kentucky moved to intervene in defense of the rule. This is likely headed to the Supreme Court. Recommend resolving the overlap with the vehicle-standards entry before using both in the same debate context, to avoid double-counting the same action.

sources.name: US EPA — Final Rule: Rescission of the Greenhouse Gas Endangerment Finding and Motor Vehicle Greenhouse Gas Emission Standards
World Resources Institute — EPA's Endangerment Finding Repeal, Explained
Davis Polk — EPA's Endangerment Finding in danger: Key takeaways and uncertainties of repeal
Federal Register — Rescission of the Greenhouse Gas Endangerment Finding and Motor Vehicle GHG Emission Standards

short_summary: Rescinded the legal basis for EPA to regulate greenhouse gases at all; vehicle standards repealed too.

rule_name: 10-for-1 Regulatory Repeal Mandate (Executive Order 14192, "Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation")
agency: White House / OMB (governs all federal agencies)
original_rule_summary: No prior rule -- this is a new process mandate. It supersedes the first Trump administration's 2017 EO 13771, which required a 2-for-1 repeal ratio.
what_changed: Requires every federal agency to identify at least 10 existing regulations (broadly defined to include guidance documents, memoranda, and policy statements, not just formal APA rules) to repeal for every 1 new regulation proposed. Also requires that the total incremental cost of all new regulations in FY2025 be 'significantly less than zero,' effectively mandating net deregulation by volume and cost simultaneously. Exempts military, national security, homeland security, foreign affairs, and immigration-related regulations.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Signed personally on January 31, 2025, his first full month in office in the second term; explicitly builds on and escalates his own first-term EO 13771 (2-for-1).

corporate_beneficiaries: Regulated industries broadly across all sectors -- this is a cross-cutting structural mandate rather than a sector-specific rollback

estimated_health_impact.summary: Severe systemic/structural risk: by design, this EO prioritizes repeal volume and aggregate cost reduction over case-by-case risk assessment of which specific protections get cut, creating pressure to eliminate rules based on their cost to industry rather than their public health or safety value.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: No quantified health impact figure exists for the EO itself, since it is a process mandate rather than a rule targeting a specific pollutant, product, or practice -- its harm is best argued structurally (the mechanism itself deprioritizes public-health benefit analysis) rather than with a body-count claim. Downstream rules repealed under its authority may have their own quantified figures (see other entries in this tracker).

confidence_note: This is one of the strongest 'process is the harm' entries in the tracker -- the EO's text itself, not a critic's characterization, establishes that repeal volume/cost savings are the stated goal, independent of the safety value of any given rule. Good for framing a systemic argument distinct from any single rollback.

sources.name: Federal Register — Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation (EO 14192)
US EPA — Executive Order 14192
Sabin Center for Climate Change Law, Columbia University — Trump Administration Institutes "10-to-1" Deregulation Rule

short_summary: Agencies must repeal 10 existing rules for every 1 new rule they issue.

rule_name: Risk Management Program 'Common Sense Approach' Rollback
agency: EPA (Office of Land and Emergency Management)
status: Proposed (comment period closed May 11, 2026; EPA has stated intent to finalize in late 2026)
original_rule_summary: The 2024 Safer Communities by Chemical Accident Prevention (SCCAP) rule, finalized under the Biden EPA, strengthened Risk Management Program (RMP) requirements under Clean Air Act Section 112(r) for roughly 11,700-11,900 facilities handling extremely hazardous substances. Key provisions included mandatory Safer Technology and Alternatives Analyses (STAA) with implementation requirements for high-accident-rate sectors (chemical manufacturing, petroleum/coal products), third-party compliance audits, expanded public disclosure of chemical hazard information, natural-hazard and power-loss risk assessment requirements, worker protections including anonymous hazard reporting and stop-work authority, and enhanced community/emergency-responder notification systems.
what_changed: On February 24, 2026, EPA published a proposed rule, the 'Common Sense Approach to Chemical Accident Prevention,' that would rescind or substantially scale back most of the 2024 SCCAP requirements. Key rollbacks: eliminating STAA requirements for existing facilities in the two highest-risk sectors (chemical manufacturing and petroleum/coal products, NAICS 324/325), limiting STAA to newly built processes only; rescinding most third-party audit requirements (with a narrower option requiring audits only for facilities with 2+ accidents in 5 years); eliminating the requirement to share chemical hazard information directly with the public on request, relying instead on EPA's more limited online Data Tool; and rescinding several worker-protection provisions including anonymous reporting, stop-work authority, and mandatory workers' rights training. EPA frames the changes as realigning RMP with OSHA's existing Process Safety Management standard and removing 'duplicative' requirements. The proposal followed a March 2025 EPA announcement that it would reconsider the SCCAP rule, itself prompted by Executive Orders 14148 and 14154 directing agencies to rescind Biden-era regulatory actions. The public comment period closed May 11, 2026; as of this writing the rule remains a proposal, with EPA's own regulatory agenda indicating a target of finalizing it in late 2026.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: Announced on March 12, 2025 that EPA would reconsider the 2024 SCCAP rule; EPA under his leadership published the Common Sense Approach proposed rule in February 2026 and held the required public hearing in March 2026.

corporate_beneficiaries: Roughly 11,700-11,900 RMP-regulated facilities nationwide, concentrated in chemical manufacturing and petroleum/coal products refining -- sectors represented in commenting by the American Chemistry Council, American Fuel & Petrochemical Manufacturers, American Petroleum Institute, and the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, all of which had petitioned against the 2024 rule before this rollback was proposed.

estimated_health_impact.summary: EPA's own 2024 SCCAP rulemaking found accidental releases from RMP facilities cost society more than $540 million annually and cited a real-world case (the 2019 TPC Group explosion in Port Neches, TX) where the STAA and safeguard requirements being rolled back likely would have mitigated or prevented the accident. EPA's own estimated population near RMP facilities is disproportionately exposed to accident risk.

figures.metric: People living within 3 miles of an RMP-regulated facility (per EPA's 2024 rulemaking)
figures.value: approximately 131 million, including about 20 million who identify as Black or African American and 32 million who identify as Hispanic or Latino
figures.source: EPA news release, 'EPA finalizes stronger safety standards to protect at-risk communities from chemical accidents,' 2024

figures.metric: Annual societal cost of RMP-facility accidental releases (EPA's 2024 estimate)
figures.source: EPA 2024 SCCAP rule news release

figures.metric: EPA's own projected annualized cost savings from the 2026 rollback if finalized
figures.source: EPA Fact Sheet, Common Sense Approach to Chemical Accident Prevention Proposed Rule

figures.metric: RMP-reportable accidents, 2014 vs. 2023 (EPA's cited justification for the rollback)
figures.source: Holland & Knight legal alert, citing EPA's proposed-rule preamble

estimated_health_impact.caveat: EPA's stated rationale is that RMP-reportable accidents already declined substantially (147 to 81, 2014-2023) before the 2024 SCCAP requirements took effect, which the agency argues shows the prior, less stringent framework was already working, and that realigning with OSHA's existing PSM standard removes genuine duplication rather than protection. Public health, labor, and environmental groups (Union of Concerned Scientists, various labor unions who testified at the March 2026 hearing) dispute that the accident-decline trend justifies removing forward-looking safeguards like natural-hazard assessment, given the increasing frequency of hurricanes and extreme weather cited as a specific risk factor. This entry describes a proposed, not yet finalized, rule -- EPA has stated an intent to finalize in late 2026, and the specific provisions could still change before then.

confidence_note: High confidence on the rule's content, timeline, and both sides' stated rationale, sourced to EPA's own Federal Register notice and fact sheet plus multiple independent law-firm summaries (Holland & Knight, Holland & Hart, DLA Piper, Beveridge & Diamond, Haynes Boone) that are consistent with each other on the substantive changes. The cost-savings and accident-decline figures are EPA's own; the population and past-cost figures are EPA's own from the prior (2024) rulemaking it is now rolling back, which is a useful internal-consistency data point. Because this is a proposed rather than final rule, `status` and the specific provisions should be rechecked before EPA's expected late-2026 final action.

US EPA -- Fact Sheet: Common Sense Approach to Chemical Accident Prevention Proposed Rule
US EPA -- EPA finalizes stronger safety standards to protect at-risk communities from chemical accidents (2024 SCCAP news release)
Holland & Knight -- EPA's Proposed Rule Signals Rollback of Chemical Safety Requirements as Key Questions Remain
Manko, Gold, Katcher & Fox -- Risk Management Program Rule: What Changed and What's Ahead in 2026
UCS Blog -- EPA, 'Common Sense' is Protecting Communities and Workers from Chemical Disasters (critical perspective, includes public hearing testimony)

rule_name: FCC Net Neutrality Repeal ("Restoring Internet Freedom Order")
original_rule_summary: 2015 FCC Open Internet Order reclassified broadband internet access as a Title II common-carrier telecommunications service, banning internet service providers from blocking, throttling, or paid-prioritizing web traffic based on source, destination, or content.
what_changed: The FCC voted 3-2 along party lines to reclassify broadband back to a lightly-regulated Title I 'information service,' eliminating the common-carrier blocking/throttling/prioritization bans. ISPs remain subject only to a transparency (disclosure) requirement.

primary_proponent.name: Ajit Pai
primary_proponent.note: Led the repeal vote; had dissented against the original 2015 rules and argued net neutrality regulation was solving a problem that didn't exist ('the Internet wasn't broken in 2015').

corporate_beneficiaries: Major broadband/telecom providers (cable and telecom ISPs) gaining ability to prioritize, throttle, or charge for tiered access to web traffic

estimated_health_impact.summary: No direct physical health impact in most cases. The most-cited health-adjacent concern is that ISPs could theoretically throttle or deprioritize telehealth traffic (video visits, remote monitoring data) the same way they could any other non-priority data, though this has not been the primary documented real-world harm since the repeal.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: This is fundamentally a digital-access/market-structure issue, not a health rollback in the sense of the environmental or labor entries in this tracker -- use the telehealth-throttling angle cautiously as a theoretical risk, not a documented realized harm.

confidence_note: Very well-documented, uncontroversial factually (clear vote, clear date, clear named proponent). Weakest entry in the tracker for a health-impact argument specifically -- best used for a consumer/market-access argument, not paired with the health-focused entries.

sources.name: NPR — Net Neutrality Rules: FCC Votes To Repeal Restrictions On Internet Providers
FCC — Remarks of Chairman Ajit Pai on Restoring Internet Freedom

short_summary: Let ISPs block, throttle, or prioritize internet traffic without restriction.

rule_name: FTC "Click to Cancel" / Negative Option Rule Repeal
original_rule_summary: 2024 FTC amendment to the Negative Option Rule requiring that canceling a subscription be as simple as signing up for one ('click to cancel'), targeting dark patterns and deceptive billing practices used by subscription-based businesses.
what_changed: Following a federal court vacatur, the FTC formally revised the Negative Option Rule to recodify it back to its pre-2024 text, eliminating the simplified-cancellation requirement. This was done in the same Federal Register action that also withdrew the FTC's CARS Rule (auto retail scams) and removed the Non-Compete Rule.

primary_proponent.name: Andrew Ferguson
primary_proponent.note: Same FTC leadership and general deregulatory posture as the noncompete rollback; specific individual statements on this particular rule were not found this session.

corporate_beneficiaries: Subscription-based businesses (streaming, media, retail, gyms) that benefit from friction in the cancellation process

estimated_health_impact.summary: No direct physical health impact. The harm is financial/consumer-protection: increased billing friction, deceptive-cancellation vulnerability, and financial stress from difficulty canceling unwanted recurring charges.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: This entry is a financial/consumer-protection harm, not a health harm in the sense used elsewhere in this tracker -- keep framing accordingly and don't overstate it as a health rollback.

confidence_note: Solid on the facts (Federal Register citation, clear timeline), but this is the weakest entry in the tracker for a health-impact argument specifically, since there is no health angle at all -- use it for a financial/consumer-harm argument, not alongside the environmental/labor entries in a 'health impact' framing.

sources.name: Federal Register — Revision of the Negative Option Rule, Withdrawal of the CARS Rule, Removal of the Non-Compete Rule

short_summary: Removed the requirement that canceling a subscription be as easy as signing up.

rule_name: FTC Noncompete Clause Ban Reversal
status: Fully Repealed (via withdrawal of appeal, formal rule removal 2026-02-12)
original_rule_summary: April 2024 FTC rule (under Chair Lina Khan) banning nearly all post-employment noncompete agreements nationwide, requiring employers to rescind existing clauses and notify workers, with narrow exceptions (e.g. business-sale agreements). Developed over years with 26,000 public comments, 25,000 of which supported the categorical ban.
what_changed: After district courts in Texas and Florida vacated the rule as exceeding FTC authority, the FTC voted 3-1 to withdraw its appeals and formally accede to the vacatur, abandoning the nationwide ban in favor of case-by-case enforcement against specific anticompetitive noncompete agreements. The rule was formally removed from the Code of Federal Regulations effective 2026-02-12.

primary_proponent.name: Andrew Ferguson
primary_proponent.note: Led the 3-1 vote to withdraw the appeals; had dissented against the original 2024 rule as a Commissioner, calling it unlawful.

corporate_beneficiaries: Employers broadly, particularly in industries with heavy noncompete use (healthcare, skilled trades) seeking to retain workforce restrictions

estimated_health_impact.summary: Indirect: reduced job mobility correlates with wage stagnation and, per some economic literature, worse healthcare access when workers are locked into jobs. No direct physical health pathway or quantified figure exists.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: This is a labor-mobility/economic harm claim, not a health claim in the environmental-rollback sense -- keep the framing accordingly. Commissioner Rebecca Slaughter's dissent is a useful sourced counter-voice noting the scale of public support for the original rule (25,000 of 26,000 comments).

confidence_note: Strong on the facts of the action itself (well-documented, named proponent, clear timeline). The health/wellbeing impact argument is necessarily indirect -- don't overstate it as a physical-health rollback comparable to the environmental entries in this tracker.

sources.name: Federal Trade Commission — Files to Accede to Vacatur of Non-Compete Clause Rule
Squire Patton Boggs — Rule Abandoned, Crackdown Continues
Steptoe — FTC Abandons National Noncompete Rule

short_summary: Abandoned the nationwide ban on employee noncompete agreements.

rule_name: GIPSA Farmer Fair Practices Rules Withdrawal
agency: USDA (Grain Inspection, Packers and Stockyards Administration)
original_rule_summary: December 2016 USDA/GIPSA rules (an interim final rule plus two proposed rules) developed under Agriculture Secretary Tom Vilsack, based on over 60,000 public comments, that would have made it easier for farmers -- particularly contract poultry growers -- to prove Packers and Stockyards Act violations without having to demonstrate market-wide harm, only individualized harm to themselves. Aimed at curbing retaliatory practices by meatpackers/processors against farmers who organized or complained.
what_changed: The Trump-era USDA withdrew the Farmer Fair Practices Rules shortly after they were finalized, before the interim final rule's protections took effect. USDA was subsequently sued by advocacy groups alleging the withdrawal violated the Administrative Procedure Act's requirement that agency decisions be based on 'reasoned consideration.'

primary_proponent.name: USDA leadership under the first Trump administration
primary_proponent.note: A single named individual most responsible for the specific withdrawal decision was not clearly identified in sourcing found this session -- flag as lower-confidence on individual attribution; USDA Secretary at the time was Sonny Perdue, though his personal role in this specific decision was not confirmed.

corporate_beneficiaries: Large meatpacking and poultry processing companies (the 'Big Four' controlling roughly half the broiler and turkey markets per USDA's own 2016 figures)

estimated_health_impact.summary: The claim in earlier drafts of this tracker linking this withdrawal to elevated rural agricultural suicide rates is NOT supported by any source found during verification -- do not use that claim. What is sourced: the withdrawal removed protections against retaliatory practices (e.g. processors providing bad feed or high-mortality-rate birds to growers who organize or complain), which plausibly affects farm income and negotiating leverage for independent contract growers, but no epidemiological or economic study connecting this specific withdrawal to a quantified health or mortality outcome was found.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: This entry originally included an unsourced rural-suicide-rate claim, which has been removed after verification found no supporting source. Use only the sourced economic/negotiating-leverage framing; do not reintroduce a health-mortality claim without a real epidemiological citation.

confidence_note: The regulatory action itself is well-sourced. Two things need caution: (1) the primary proponent is not a clearly identified individual -- verify further before naming one in debate; (2) the original health-impact framing (rural suicide rates) is unsupported and should not be used.

sources.name: National Agricultural Law Center — USDA sued for pulling GIPSA Farmer Fair Practices Rules
USDA — Announces Farmer Fair Practices Rules (original 2016 rule announcement, for baseline context)

short_summary: Withdrew protections letting farmers challenge unfair meatpacker practices.

rule_name: Joint-Employer Standard Narrowing (NLRB)
administration: Trump II (2025-present) -- corrected from an earlier draft that misdated this as a First Term action
status: Fully Repealed (2023 rule withdrawn; 2020 standard reinstated)
original_rule_summary: The NLRB's joint-employer standard determines when two companies (e.g. a franchisor and franchisee, or a company and its staffing agency) can both be held liable for labor law violations and both required to bargain collectively with the same workers. A 2023 Biden-era rule broadened the standard to include 'indirect' or 'reserved' (even unexercised) control over terms like wages, work rules, or health and safety -- making it easier to hold parent companies/franchisors jointly liable.
what_changed: The 2023 rule was vacated by a federal court in March 2024 as 'arbitrary and capricious.' The NLRB (with its quorum restored) formally withdrew the 2023 rule and reinstated the narrower 2020 first-Trump-term standard: an entity is now a joint employer only if it 'possesses and exercises substantial direct and immediate control' over essential terms of employment (wages, benefits, hours, hiring, discharge, discipline, supervision, direction). Indirect or unexercised control no longer counts.

primary_proponent.name: National Labor Relations Board (agency-level action)
primary_proponent.note: No single named Board member or Chairman was clearly identified as the primary individual driver in sourcing found this session -- the NLRB characterized the rulemaking as 'ministerial,' implementing the court's vacatur rather than a new policy choice by named leadership. Flag attribution as agency-level, not individual, until further verified.

corporate_beneficiaries: Franchisors, staffing agencies, and businesses using subcontractors -- explicitly named as beneficiaries in legal analysis of the rule

estimated_health_impact.summary: Minimal direct physical health impact. The harm is corporate accountability: narrowing joint-employer liability limits the ability to hold parent companies/franchisors responsible for labor violations (including workplace safety violations) occurring at a franchisee's or contractor's site, since the parent company is less likely to be considered a joint employer absent direct, exercised control.
estimated_health_impact.caveat: No quantified health/safety figure exists for this rule -- it is a liability-allocation rule, not a safety standard itself. The health-relevant argument is indirect: reduced accountability for the entity best positioned to enforce safety standards across a franchise/contractor network.

confidence_note: Solid on the facts and timeline (Federal Register citation, clear before/after standard). Two things to flag: (1) this is a Trump II action from Feb. 2026, not Trump I as an earlier version of this tracker listed -- the 2020 rule referenced here was Trump I, but the specific rollback being tracked (reversing the 2023 expansion) happened this year; (2) no individual proponent is clearly named in sourcing, unlike most other entries in this tracker.

sources.name: Federal Register — Withdrawal of 2023 Standard for Determining Joint Employer Status
Sullivan & Cromwell LLP — NLRB Reinstates Narrower Joint-Employer Standard
Jackson Lewis — NLRB Goes Back to the Employer-Friendly Future

short_summary: Made it harder to hold parent companies liable for franchisee labor violations.

rule_name: Light- and Medium-Duty Vehicle Greenhouse Gas Standards (Model Year 2027+)
original_rule_summary: EPA tailpipe emissions standards for new light- and medium-duty vehicles beginning model year 2027, designed to cut vehicle greenhouse gas emissions and accelerate the market shift toward more efficient and electric vehicles; also produced fuel-cost savings for individual drivers.
what_changed: EPA fully repealed the standards, citing Executive Order 14,154. The repeal removes both the emissions limits and the associated fuel-economy-driven consumer savings that came with them.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: Announced the broader deregulatory package (31 actions including this one) as, in EPA's own words, the 'biggest deregulatory action in U.S. history.'

corporate_beneficiaries: Automakers seeking to delay electrification investment
Oil and gas producers/refiners

estimated_health_impact.summary: This is the single largest rollback by dollar value in the Institute for Policy Integrity's tracker of 2025-2026 Trump-era energy/environmental rollbacks, combining lost consumer fuel savings, lost health benefits from air pollution reduction, and lost climate benefits.

figures.metric: Annual Net Benefits at Risk
figures.source: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law (Regulatory Rollback Tracker, updated 2026-04-20)

figures.metric: Annual Net Consumer Savings at Risk
figures.source: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law

figures.metric: Annual Monetized Health Benefits at Risk
figures.source: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law

figures.metric: Annual Premature Mortalities at Risk
figures.source: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law

estimated_health_impact.caveat: Institute for Policy Integrity is an NYU academic policy center, not a neutral government source, but its methodology is built from the agencies' own prior regulatory impact analyses of the rules being repealed -- cite it as 'built from EPA's own prior cost-benefit analysis of the standard being repealed' rather than as an independent outside estimate.

confidence_note: Strong, quantified, and traceable to agency-originated modeling (the underlying numbers trace back to EPA's own RIA for the rule before repeal). Good headline figure for debate use: 2,577 estimated annual premature mortalities at risk is the largest single figure in the entire current-term tracker.

sources.name: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law — Tracking the Damages of Regulatory Rollbacks

short_summary: Repealed vehicle emissions standards for new cars, trucks, and SUVs.

rule_name: Mercury and Air Toxics Standards (MATS) — 2026 Repeal of 2024 Amendments
original_rule_summary: 2024 Biden-era EPA strengthening of the original 2012 MATS rule, which tightened the mercury emissions limit for lignite-fired power plant units from 4.0 lb/TBtu to 1.2 lb/TBtu and added continuous particulate matter monitoring requirements.
what_changed: EPA finalized a full repeal of the 2024 strengthening, reverting the lignite-unit mercury limit back to the original 2012 baseline of 4.0 lb/TBtu and eliminating the continuous PM monitoring requirement added in 2024. This is a distinct, more recent action from the 2020 MATS cost-benefit finding reversal (see separate entry mats-cost-benefit-rollback-trump1), which addressed the rule's legal justification rather than its numeric limits.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: Signed the final repeal rule.

corporate_beneficiaries: Coal-fired power plant operators, particularly lignite-fired units

estimated_health_impact.summary: The original 2012 MATS rule was credited with a roughly 90% cut in power-plant mercury emissions and up to 11,000 lives saved annually. This repeal reverses the 2024 strengthening and allows lignite-fired units to emit more than double the mercury permitted under the 2024 standard. EPA's own 2024 Regulatory Impact Analysis for the standards now being repealed estimated $300 million/year in health benefits and $130 million/year in climate benefits, against $860 million in total compliance costs to industry between 2028-2037. Notably, EPA's 2026 RIA for the repeal itself declines to monetize the health benefits being given up.

figures.metric: EPA's own 2024 estimate of annual health benefits from the now-repealed standards
figures.source: EPA 2024 Regulatory Impact Analysis, cited by Inside Climate News

figures.metric: EPA's own 2024 estimate of annual climate benefits from the now-repealed standards
figures.source: EPA 2024 Regulatory Impact Analysis, cited by Inside Climate News

figures.metric: Total industry compliance cost estimate, 2028-2037
figures.source: EPA 2024 Regulatory Impact Analysis, cited by Inside Climate News

estimated_health_impact.caveat: These figures come from EPA's own 2024 analysis of the standards being repealed, not an outside advocacy estimate -- strong for debate use. EPA's own 2026 RIA for the repeal itself notably declines to monetize the health benefits being forfeited, which is itself a useful procedural point (an agency choosing not to quantify what it's giving up).

confidence_note: Strong on the facts of the rule change itself (EPA.gov primary source, clear before/after numeric limits) and now strong on health impact too, with EPA's own prior cost-benefit figures. The 'EPA's new RIA declines to monetize health benefits' point remains a good secondary procedural argument.

sources.name: US EPA — Mercury and Air Toxics Standards (MATS) rule page
Earthjustice — EPA Dismantles Protections for Mercury and Air Toxics From Power Plants
Inside Climate News — Without Weighing Costs to Public Health, EPA Rolls Back Air Pollution Standards for Coal Plants
Harvard EELP — Mercury and Air Toxics Standards tracker

short_summary: Reverted coal plant mercury limits to 2012 levels, undoing 2024 tightening.

rule_name: Mercury and Air Toxics Standards (MATS) — cost-benefit finding reversal
status: Weakened (legal underpinning removed, standards technically left in place)
original_rule_summary: 2012 Obama-era EPA rule requiring coal- and oil-fired power plants to limit emissions of mercury and other toxic air pollutants; credited with reducing mercury pollution from coal plants by over 80%.
what_changed: EPA finalized a reversal of the 'appropriate and necessary' cost-benefit finding underlying MATS, arguing the rule's compliance costs ($9.6 billion/year) outweighed its directly monetized benefits ($4-6 million/year in mercury-specific benefits), while excluding the much larger 'co-benefits' from reduced particulate matter exposure from that calculation. The numeric emissions standards were left technically in place, but the legal foundation was weakened, exposing the rule to future rollback or non-enforcement.

primary_proponent.name: Andrew Wheeler
primary_proponent.note: Signed and announced the 2020 finding reversal; a former coal industry lobbyist (Murray Energy) prior to joining EPA.

corporate_beneficiaries: Coal-fired power plant operators
Murray Energy and other coal producers

estimated_health_impact.summary: By excluding co-benefits from particulate matter reduction, the reversal undercut the primary public-health justification for the rule -- particulate matter co-benefits accounted for the large majority of MATS's total estimated $37-90 billion in annual benefits versus roughly $9.6 billion in annual compliance costs.

figures.metric: EPA's original monetized total annual benefits (incl. co-benefits)
figures.source: 2011 EPA Regulatory Impact Analysis for MATS

figures.metric: EPA's original monetized annual compliance costs
figures.source: 2011 EPA Regulatory Impact Analysis for MATS

estimated_health_impact.caveat: The 2020 finding reversal did not itself change emissions in the short term since numeric standards stayed in place; the health risk is prospective -- it removed the legal basis that makes the standards defensible against future non-enforcement or repeal.

confidence_note: Strong, agency's-own-numbers framing: the cost-benefit dispute is about whether to count co-benefits the EPA itself originally calculated, not a competing outside estimate. Good for a standards-based debate framing -- ask why co-benefits were excluded only for this rule.

sources.name: Brookings — The Trump administration's major environmental deregulations
NRDC — How EPA Rollbacks Will Cost Us

short_summary: Excluded major health co-benefits from the cost-benefit case for mercury limits.

rule_name: MSHA silica exposure rule reconsideration/pause and NIOSH functional elimination
agency: Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) / National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH), Dept. of Labor and HHS respectively
status: Delayed/Paused, escalating toward reconsideration rulemaking (silica rule); Weakened to point of functional elimination (NIOSH)
date_of_action: 2025-04-08 (initial enforcement pause); litigation and reconsideration process ongoing as of mid-2025 through at least Oct 2025
original_rule_summary: A new MSHA silica exposure rule (finalized April 18, 2024 under the Biden administration; effective June 17, 2024) would halve the allowable exposure limit to respirable crystalline silica dust (to 50 micrograms/cubic meter) for coal and metal/nonmetal (MNM) miners, requiring enhanced monitoring, medical surveillance, and respiratory protection, targeting silicosis, black lung disease, and other respiratory illness. Separately, NIOSH is the federal agency that conducts the injury/fatality research and certifies respiratory protection equipment underlying OSHA and MSHA rulemaking.
what_changed: MSHA announced an enforcement pause for coal mine operators on April 8, 2025 (initially through Aug 18, 2025), citing 'unforeseen NIOSH restructuring' affecting the supply of certified respirators and dust monitors. Separately and significantly, the 8th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals granted industry groups (National Stone, Sand & Gravel Association; National Mining Association) a temporary stay of the rule on April 4, 2025, before MSHA's own pause was announced. By around October 2025, MSHA announced it would 'engage in limited rulemaking to reconsider' portions of the rule under ongoing litigation -- a materially stronger action than an administrative enforcement pause, since it signals MSHA intends to substantively rewrite parts of the rule, not just delay its start date. The metal/nonmetal (MNM) compliance deadline of April 8, 2026 has now passed as of this entry's last verification; this entry's sourcing does not confirm whether that deadline was met, further paused, or is itself subject to the same reconsideration process -- flag for follow-up. Separately, the administration's restructuring eliminated significant NIOSH research capacity (reported layoffs of 870+ NIOSH employees) and closed 11 OSHA field offices in states with the highest workplace fatality rates, plus 34 MSHA offices.

primary_proponent.name: Department of Labor leadership under the Trump II administration
primary_proponent.role: MSHA and OSHA are both housed under DOL
primary_proponent.note: Reporting on the specific individual driving the NIOSH cuts ties them to the administration's broader federal workforce restructuring effort (DOGE-linked); a single named proponent for this specific action was not clearly identified in available sourcing -- flag as lower-confidence on attribution and verify before using in debate. The litigation itself was initiated by industry petitioners (NSSGA, National Mining Association), not the administration, though MSHA's subsequent reconsideration rulemaking is an administration action responding to that litigation.

corporate_beneficiaries: Coal and metal/nonmetal mining operators subject to the paused/reconsidered stricter silica exposure limit
National Stone, Sand & Gravel Association and National Mining Association member companies specifically, as the litigants who obtained the initial stay

estimated_health_impact.summary: The Department of Labor's own prior estimate for the silica rule (before the pause/reconsideration) projected substantial prevented deaths and illness -- pausing enforcement and reconsidering the rule puts those prevented cases at risk. Notably, a bipartisan-leaning congressional signal exists on the other side: the Senate Appropriations Committee approved report language (July 31, 2025) directing MSHA to 'fully and effectively enforce' the rule and not use funds to weaken it -- worth citing as a counterpoint showing this is contested even within Congress, not a uniformly one-sided rollback.

figures.metric: DOL's own estimate of deaths prevented by the (now-paused/reconsidered) silica rule
figures.source: Department of Labor rule estimate, cited by Economic Policy Institute

figures.metric: DOL's own estimate of silica-related illness cases prevented
figures.source: Department of Labor rule estimate, cited by Economic Policy Institute

estimated_health_impact.caveat: These figures are the Department of Labor's own original estimate for the benefit of the rule now being paused/reconsidered (agency's own numbers, strong for debate), as reported secondhand via EPI -- worth verifying against the original DOL rule text directly before using as a primary citation.

confidence_note: Primary proponent attribution remains the weak point -- flag it and don't name a specific individual with high confidence until verified. The health impact figures are agency-originated and strong. Status has escalated since last verification: this is no longer just an administrative 'pause' but an active reconsideration rulemaking under litigation pressure, which is a materially stronger claim about the rule's vulnerability. The passed MNM compliance deadline (April 8, 2026) needs a follow-up check to determine current enforcement status for that sector specifically.

sources.name: Economic Policy Institute — Too many workers die on the job every year. Trump's attacks on OSHA will kill more.
Safety+Health Magazine — MSHA to 'reconsider' rule on protecting miners from silica exposure
Fisher Phillips LLP — MSHA's Silica Rule Enforcement on Pause After Court Blocks Effective Date
Safety+Health Magazine — Lawmakers push for changes to MSHA's silica rule

short_summary: Paused stricter mine silica dust limits and cut federal mine-safety research capacity.

rule_name: OSHA 'Improve Tracking of Workplace Injuries and Illnesses' rule — electronic reporting rollback
original_rule_summary: 2016 Obama-era OSHA rule requiring employers with 250+ workers at an establishment to electronically submit detailed, itemized workplace injury and illness data (Forms 300, 301, 300A) to OSHA annually, making injury patterns easier for regulators, researchers, and the public to identify.
what_changed: OSHA finalized a rule eliminating the requirement to submit the detailed itemized logs (Forms 300 and 301), leaving only the aggregated summary form (300A). The final rule was published during the 2018-2019 government shutdown. Anti-retaliation protections for injury reporting were left in place.

primary_proponent.name: Alexander Acosta
primary_proponent.note: Department of Labor under Acosta finalized and published the rule; OSHA is a sub-agency of DOL.

corporate_beneficiaries: Large employers (250+ workers) previously subject to detailed injury-log disclosure, particularly in construction and manufacturing

estimated_health_impact.summary: Labor advocates and unions argued the rollback makes it significantly harder for OSHA, researchers, and the public to detect workplace-injury patterns and target enforcement, since only aggregate (not itemized, establishment-level) data remains available -- an effect on detection and prevention capacity rather than a directly modeled mortality/injury count.

figures.metric: U.S. workplace deaths per year (context figure, not rollback-specific)
figures.source: Economic Policy Institute, citing BLS data

estimated_health_impact.caveat: No agency or independent body has published a quantified estimate of additional deaths/injuries directly attributable to this specific reporting rollback -- the harm argument is about degraded detection/prevention capacity, not a modeled body count. Present it that way rather than implying a specific casualty figure.

confidence_note: Frame as a transparency/detection-capacity rollback, not a body-count claim -- this is the more defensible, harder-to-rebut version of the argument. Unions (IBEW) and NELP are the sourced critics; note them as advocacy voices, not neutral analysts.

sources.name: National Employment Law Project — Trump Administration Rolls Back Workplace Injury Reporting Rule
UCOMM Blog — Trump Repeals OSHA Safety Standards
The Hill — OSHA to reduce Obama-era injury report requirements for large companies

short_summary: Ended detailed public reporting of workplace injuries at large employers.

rule_name: PFAS Drinking Water Rule Rollback (Compliance Extension + Partial Rescission)
status: Proposed Repeal (two linked proposed rules, comment period closed 2026-07-20, public hearing 2026-07-07, not yet finalized)
original_rule_summary: April 2024 EPA National Primary Drinking Water Regulation -- the first-ever federal limits on PFAS ('forever chemicals') in drinking water -- set legally enforceable Maximum Contaminant Levels (MCLs) for six PFAS (PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFNA, HFPO-DA/GenX, and Hazard Index mixtures), with compliance required by 2029. EPA's own analysis projected the rule would prevent PFAS exposure for approximately 100 million people and prevent thousands of deaths and tens of thousands of serious illnesses over time.
what_changed: EPA proposed two linked rules: (1) rescinding enforceable limits entirely for four of the six regulated PFAS (PFHxS, PFNA, HFPO-DA/GenX, and the Hazard Index mixture), while keeping the PFOA/PFOS limits (4 ppt) technically in place; and (2) extending the PFOA/PFOS compliance deadline by two years, from 2029 to 2031, via a federal exemption framework. Net effect: of the six PFAS regulated in 2024, only two retain enforceable limits, and even those get a longer compliance runway.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: Personally announced the plan in April/May 2025; EPA frames the rescission as correcting a procedural 'failure to follow the clear statutory requirements' of the Safe Drinking Water Act rather than a substantive health disagreement, and states it is 'not seeking comment on... any information about health risks associated with PFAS.'

corporate_beneficiaries: Water utilities facing compliance costs
PFAS manufacturers and industrial users of the four PFAS losing enforceable limits

estimated_health_impact.summary: EPA's own original 2024 rule projected the full six-PFAS rule would prevent PFAS exposure for ~100 million people and prevent thousands of deaths and tens of thousands of serious illnesses over time -- this is the benefit now partially being unwound by removing 4 of 6 chemicals from enforceable limits and delaying compliance on the remaining 2 by two years. PFAS exposure is linked by CDC/ATSDR research to certain cancers, thyroid disruption, immune effects in children, reproductive difficulties, and elevated cholesterol.

figures.metric: People EPA's 2024 rule was projected to protect from PFAS exposure
figures.source: US EPA, 2024 NPDWR announcement

figures.metric: PFAS compounds losing enforceable federal limits under the 2026 proposal
figures.value: 4 of 6 (PFHxS, PFNA, HFPO-DA, Hazard Index mixture)
figures.source: US EPA, May 2026 proposed rules

estimated_health_impact.caveat: As of this entry, both actions are proposed, not finalized -- comment period closed July 20, 2026. EPA states explicitly it is not accepting comment on the underlying health-risk science, only on the legal/procedural basis for rescission -- worth noting this framing choice in debate, since it forecloses public health argument at the comment stage.

confidence_note: Strong, EPA's-own-numbers framing available (the ~100 million people figure comes from EPA's own 2024 rule justification, not an outside estimate). Note carefully that this is still proposed, not finalized, and litigation is expected regardless of outcome per legal analysts. [Rechecked 2026-07-06: no material change -- comment period closes July 20, 2026 (as previously noted, confirming those forward-looking dates were accurate, not an error); public hearing held July 7, 2026; EPA states intent to finalize both rules before end of 2026.]

short_summary: Proposed dropping enforceable limits on 4 of 6 regulated 'forever chemicals.'

rule_name: Power Plant Greenhouse Gas Standards (Clean Air Act Section 111 EGU Standards)
status: Proposed Repeal (final rule sent to OMB for review as of 2026-05-14, not yet published)
date_of_action: 2025-06-11 (proposed); sent to OMB/OIRA for final review 2026-05-14
original_rule_summary: EPA carbon dioxide emissions standards for fossil-fuel-fired power plants (coal and new gas), issued under Clean Air Act Section 111, requiring plants to install carbon capture or otherwise limit CO2 output. Finalized by the Biden administration May 9, 2024.
what_changed: EPA proposed repealing the standards entirely on June 11, 2025, citing Executive Order 14,154 and arguing GHG emissions from fossil-fuel-fired power plants do not 'contribute significantly' to dangerous air pollution. As of May 14, 2026, EPA sent a final rule to the White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB) for review -- the last procedural step before publication in the Federal Register. OMB reviews can take up to 90 days, meaning publication could occur any time through roughly mid-August 2026. This is a status escalation from the prior 'proposed, no further movement noted' state -- the rule is now genuinely close to finalization, though not yet finalized as of this entry's last verification.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: Part of the same broader deregulatory package targeting power plant pollution rules and the endangerment finding. The related Endangerment Finding rescission (see separate entry) finalized Feb 12, 2026 and rests on the same 'does not contribute significantly' legal theory being applied here.

corporate_beneficiaries: Coal-fired power plant operators
Fossil-gas-fired power plant developers

estimated_health_impact.summary: Second-largest single rollback in the current-term tracker by dollar value (per Institute for Policy Integrity), with the majority of monetized benefits coming from avoided climate damages, but with a substantial monetized public-health component from reduced particulate and other co-pollutant exposure near power plants. Separately, EPA's own regulatory text for the underlying proposal estimates compliance-cost savings alone (not the broader net-benefit figure below) at $19 billion (3% discount rate) or $9.6 billion (7% discount rate) in present value over the 2026-2047 period -- a narrower, agency-originated figure worth citing alongside the broader Policy Integrity estimate.

figures.metric: Annual Net Benefits at Risk
figures.source: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law (updated 2026-04-20)

figures.metric: Annual Monetized Health Benefits at Risk
figures.source: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law

figures.metric: Annual Premature Mortalities at Risk
figures.source: Institute for Policy Integrity, NYU School of Law

figures.metric: EPA's own estimated compliance-cost savings, PV 2026-2047 (3% discount rate)
figures.source: EPA proposed rule, Federal Register, June 17 2025

estimated_health_impact.caveat: Still a proposed, not finalized, repeal as of this entry's last update -- frame accordingly ('EPA has proposed, and sent a final rule to OMB for review,' not 'EPA has repealed'). The EPA's own $19B/$9.6B figures are compliance-cost savings only, a narrower measure than Policy Integrity's broader net-benefit estimate which includes health and climate costs -- don't conflate the two when citing.

confidence_note: Note the status carefully in debate use -- this one is not finalized yet, though it is now at the last procedural stage (OMB review) before publication, unlike its status at last verification. Otherwise same sourcing strength as the vehicle-standards entry. Worth a follow-up check after mid-August 2026 to see whether OMB review concluded and the rule published.
